tip: At B1, move beyond 'hi'. Master register: when to use casual vs neutral vs warm greetings makes you sound natural, not textbook.

words.sentence_en: More relaxed than 'hi'; common among friends.
words.notes: Casual. In emails 'Hey' is informal; use 'Hi' or 'Hello' for work.
words.image_prompt: two cheerful friends waving hello to each other in a sunny park

tip: Greetings carry register. 'Good morning' and 'Take care' sound warmer and more professional than a bare 'hi/bye' in many situations.

words.meaning: A polite greeting used before noon
words.sentence_ru: Good morning, everyone. Shall we begin?
words.notes: Switch to 'Good afternoon' after 12, 'Good evening' after ~6pm.
words.image_prompt: a person greeting colleagues warmly at a sunny office entrance

words.meaning: A warm way to say goodbye
words.sentence_ru: It was great seeing you. Take care!
words.notes: Warmer than 'bye'. 'Take it easy' is similar and casual.
words.image_prompt: two colleagues waving goodbye warmly at a train station

tip: Beyond yes/no: B1 speakers soften answers. 'Sure', 'Definitely', 'Not really', 'I'm afraid not' sound far more natural.

words.notes: Stronger than plain 'yes'. Also: 'Absolutely', 'For sure'.
words.image_prompt: a smiling person giving an enthusiastic thumbs up

words.meaning: A soft, polite way to say no
words.sentence_ru: "Do you like it?" "Not really, to be honest."
words.notes: Softens disagreement. 'I'm afraid not' is more formal.
words.image_prompt: a person gently shaking their head with a soft expression

tip: Politeness levels matter. 'Could you...?' and 'I'd appreciate it' sound more polished than 'please' alone at B1+.

words.notes: 'I'd appreciate it if you could...' is polite for requests.
words.image_prompt: two hands gratefully receiving a gift wrapped in ribbon

words.meaning: A polite way to make a request
words.sentence_ru: Could you pass me the salt, please?
words.notes: 'Could/Would you' soften requests vs the direct 'Can you'.
words.image_prompt: a person politely gesturing a request at a dinner table

tip: Apologies have degrees. 'My apologies' (formal), 'Sorry about that' (casual), 'I beg your pardon' (very formal) fit different settings.

words.notes: Noun: apology. 'My apologies' is a polished set phrase.
words.image_prompt: a person offering a sincere apology with open hands

words.meaning: Used to get attention or pass by
words.sentence_ru: Excuse me, is this seat taken?
words.notes: Different from 'sorry' (regret). 'Pardon me' is more formal.
words.image_prompt: a person politely getting attention in a crowded space

tip: 'How are you?' has many natural variants. 'How's it going?', 'How have you been?' sound more native than the textbook version.

words.notes: Reply: 'Pretty good, you?' Casual register.
words.image_prompt: two friends meeting on a bridge, one casually greeting the other

words.meaning: Asking about someone after time apart
words.sentence_ru: Long time no see! How have you been?
words.sentence_en: For people you haven't seen recently.
words.notes: Present perfect — used after a gap in time.
words.image_prompt: two old friends reuniting with happy expressions

tip: Subject vs object pronouns trip up many learners. 'between you and me' (not 'I'); 'It's me' in speech. B1 nails these.

words.sentence_en: Used for emphasis or reflexive actions.
words.notes: 'by myself' = alone. Don't overuse for 'me'.
words.image_prompt: a person proudly showing something they made alone

words.notes: 'Help yourself' = take freely. Common set phrase.
words.image_prompt: a person gesturing welcomingly to a guest

words.notes: 'my book' = mine. Don't say 'mine book'.
words.image_prompt: a hand claiming a personal item with a smile

tip: 'They' is now widely used as a singular, gender-neutral pronoun — useful and natural in modern English.

words.sentence_ru: Someone called, but they didn't leave a name.
words.notes: Accepted for unknown/non-binary individuals.
words.image_prompt: a diverse small group of people chatting together

words.sentence_ru: They organized the whole event themselves.
words.notes: Singular neutral: 'themself' is emerging.
words.image_prompt: a group proudly standing by something they built

words.notes: 'Let's' = 'let us'. Very common contraction.
words.image_prompt: four friends linking arms and inviting the viewer

words.notes: 'our team' → 'ours'.
words.image_prompt: a group celebrating a shared achievement

tip: Possessive 's vs of: 'the dog's tail' (living) vs 'the leg of the table' (often non-living). B1 starts feeling this nuance.

words.notes: 'its' = belonging to it; 'it's' = 'it is'. Top error!
words.image_prompt: a building with a small flag representing ownership

words.notes: 'Whose' (owns) vs 'who's' (who is). Common confusion.
words.image_prompt: a found bag with a question mark above it

tip: Family words have warm informal forms: Mom/Mum, Dad, Grandma, Grandpa. British vs American spelling differs (Mum vs Mom).

words.notes: 'parent' singular; 'relatives' = wider family.
words.image_prompt: a loving mother and father with their child in a sunny home

words.sentence_ru: They raised three kids in the countryside.
words.notes: 'raise a child' (US) / 'bring up' (UK common).
words.image_prompt: parents lovingly guiding their growing children

tip: Useful B1 family vocabulary goes beyond basics: 'sibling' (brother or sister), 'relatives', 'in-laws' add precision.

words.notes: More efficient than 'brothers or sisters'.
words.image_prompt: two siblings laughing together

words.sentence_ru: We visit our relatives every summer.
words.notes: 'relations' is also possible but less common.
words.image_prompt: an extended family gathering at a reunion

words.meaning: Child of your aunt or uncle
words.notes: English doesn't mark cousin's gender or age.
words.image_prompt: two young cousins playing in a garden

tip: 'Grandparent' is the neutral term. Affectionate forms: Grandma/Granny, Grandpa/Granddad vary by family and region.

words.sentence_ru: My grandparents taught me to cook.
words.notes: Singular: grandparent.
words.image_prompt: loving grandparents with their grandchild in a cozy home

words.notes: 'old people' can sound blunt; 'elderly' is polite.
words.image_prompt: a kind elderly couple smiling warmly on a bench

tip: English distinguishes friendship levels: 'acquaintance', 'colleague', 'close friend', 'best friend'. Precision sounds advanced.

words.sentence_ru: He's just an acquaintance, not a close friend.
words.notes: Tricky spelling! Note the 'cq'.
words.image_prompt: two people exchanging a polite, distant nod

words.notes: 'coworker' (US) is a synonym.
words.image_prompt: two coworkers collaborating at a shared desk

tip: 'Home' vs 'house': home is where you belong (emotional); house is the building. 'Make yourself at home' is a key phrase.

words.sentence_ru: Their living room is so cosy.
words.notes: Spelled 'cozy' in US English.
words.image_prompt: a warm cosy living room with soft lighting and blankets

words.meaning: To feel accepted; to be owned by
words.sentence_ru: I really feel I belong here.
words.notes: 'belong to' = owned by; 'belong here' = fit in.
words.image_prompt: a person feeling at home in a welcoming space

tip: Room vocabulary expands at B1: 'spare room', 'study', 'hallway', 'landing'. British and American house terms can differ.

words.sentence_ru: You can stay in the spare room.
words.notes: 'guest room' is a synonym.
words.image_prompt: a tidy welcoming guest bedroom with fresh linens

words.meaning: A room for working or reading
words.notes: Also a verb ('to study') — context decides.
words.image_prompt: a cozy home study with a desk and bookshelves

tip: Phrasal verbs with doors/windows are everyday gold: 'open up', 'shut', 'lock up'. B1 should collect phrasal verbs actively.

words.meaning: To secure a building by locking it
words.sentence_ru: Don't forget to lock up before you leave.
words.notes: Phrasal verb; 'lock' alone is also fine.
words.image_prompt: a hand turning a key to lock a front door at dusk

words.sentence_ru: Please shut the window; it's cold.
words.notes: 'Shut up' is rude — be careful!
words.image_prompt: an open window being closed against a chilly breeze

tip: Countable vs uncountable nouns matter at B1: 'furniture' is uncountable (a piece of furniture), unlike 'chair'.

words.meaning: Movable household items like tables, chairs
words.sentence_ru: We bought new furniture for the flat.
words.notes: Say 'a piece of furniture', never 'furnitures'.
words.image_prompt: a tastefully furnished living room with various pieces

words.sentence_ru: Please keep your belongings with you.
words.notes: Usually plural. Formal-ish; common in signs.
words.image_prompt: a neatly packed bag with personal items

tip: 'Stationery' (pens, paper) vs 'stationary' (not moving) — a classic spelling trap worth mastering at B1.

words.meaning: A book of blank pages for writing
words.sentence_ru: I jot ideas in my notebook.
words.notes: Context shows if it's paper or a computer.
words.image_prompt: an open notebook with a pen and handwritten notes

words.sentence_ru: I love browsing the stationery shop.
words.notes: 'stationery' (e) = supplies; 'stationary' (a) = still.
words.image_prompt: an array of beautiful pens, paper and writing supplies

tip: Tech vocab is full of useful phrasal verbs and collocations: 'charge', 'log in', 'back up', 'crash'. Collect these.

words.meaning: To refill a battery with power
words.sentence_ru: I need to charge my phone.
words.sentence_en: Also: a fee, or to rush at.
words.notes: 'charger' = the device. Multiple meanings!
words.image_prompt: a smartphone charging with a glowing battery icon

words.sentence_ru: This app works on any device.
words.notes: 'devise' (verb) sounds similar but means 'invent'.
words.image_prompt: various modern electronic devices on a desk

tip: Uncountable food nouns: 'bread', 'water' need 'a piece/glass of'. B1 should use quantifiers naturally.

words.sentence_ru: Could I have a glass of water?
words.notes: 'a glass of water', 'a cup of tea' — match container to drink.
words.image_prompt: a clear glass of water with droplets on a table

words.meaning: A whole shaped piece of bread
words.sentence_ru: I bought a fresh loaf of bread.
words.notes: Plural: loaves. 'a slice of bread' = one piece.
words.image_prompt: a fresh whole loaf of bread on a wooden board

tip: Drinks invite great collocations: 'a cup of tea', 'grab a coffee', 'put the kettle on' (very British!).

words.sentence_ru: Let me brew some fresh coffee.
words.sentence_en: Also a noun: a hot drink.
words.notes: 'a brew' (UK slang) = a cup of tea.
words.image_prompt: a cup of freshly brewed tea with steam rising

words.notes: 'Put the kettle on' = make tea, very British.
words.image_prompt: a classic kettle steaming on a stove

tip: Coffee culture has rich vocabulary: 'decaf', 'to go', 'refill'. Ordering fluently is a real B1 milestone.

words.meaning: Food/drink taken away, not eaten in
words.sentence_ru: A large coffee to go, please.
words.notes: 'for here' = eat in; 'to go' = take away.
words.image_prompt: a paper coffee cup ready to take away

words.meaning: A second serving of a drink
words.notes: Also a verb: to fill again.
words.image_prompt: a coffee cup being refilled from a pot

tip: Fruit idioms enrich speech: 'the apple of my eye', 'go bananas'. B1 learners enjoy figurative language.

words.meaning: Fully grown and ready to eat
words.notes: 'ripe' (ready) vs 'rotten' (spoiled).
words.image_prompt: a bunch of perfectly ripe yellow bananas

words.meaning: Recently made or picked; not stale
words.sentence_ru: I love fresh fruit in the morning.
words.notes: 'fresh' also means cheeky/rude in slang.
words.image_prompt: a colorful display of fresh fruit at a market

tip: Cooking verbs are B1 essentials: 'grill', 'roast', 'fry', 'boil'. Each implies a different method.

words.sentence_ru: We grilled fish on the barbecue.
words.notes: Noun: a grill/barbecue. 'grilled' = cooked this way.
words.image_prompt: fresh fish grilling over an open flame

words.notes: Fish 'goes off' (spoils) quickly.
words.image_prompt: a fresh fish on ice with lemon and herbs

tip: Food adjectives add flavor to speech: 'hearty', 'crisp', 'savoury'. Describing food well is a fun B1 skill.

words.sentence_ru: A hearty soup on a cold day is perfect.
words.notes: 'a hearty meal' = big and nourishing.
words.image_prompt: a steaming bowl of hearty vegetable soup

words.sentence_ru: I like a crisp green salad.
words.notes: 'crispy' = crunchy (fried); 'crisp' = fresh/firm.
words.image_prompt: a fresh crisp green salad in a bowl

tip: Describing sweets: 'rich', 'creamy', 'sickly' (too sweet). Tasteful adjectives elevate B1 conversation.

words.meaning: Something special and enjoyable, often sweet
words.sentence_ru: This cake is a real treat.
words.sentence_en: Also a verb: to treat someone.
words.notes: 'my treat' = I'll pay.
words.image_prompt: a beautiful slice of cake presented as a special treat

words.sentence_ru: I have such a sweet tooth!
words.notes: 'have a sweet tooth' = love sweets.
words.image_prompt: a happy person enjoying an assortment of sweets

tip: The verb 'to be' is irregular but vital. B1 focus: contractions ('I'm', 'they're') and tag questions ('isn't it?').

words.sentence_ru: I tend to wake up early.
words.notes: More nuanced than 'usually'.
words.image_prompt: a person following their usual morning routine

tip: 'Have' has many uses: possession, experiences ('have lunch'), and as an auxiliary ('have been'). B1 distinguishes these.

words.notes: 'my own' = belonging only to me.
words.image_prompt: a person proudly standing by something they own

words.sentence_ru: She resides in the city centre.
words.notes: Used in official/formal contexts.
words.image_prompt: a person at the window of their home

tip: English movement verbs are precise: 'commute', 'head to', 'set off'. Phrasal verbs of motion are very natural.

words.sentence_ru: I commute by train every day.
words.notes: Noun: 'a long commute'.
words.image_prompt: a person traveling to work on a morning train

words.notes: 'head off' = leave; 'head back' = return.
words.image_prompt: a person walking purposefully toward a destination

tip: 'Read' and 'write' anchor many collocations: 'read up on', 'write down', 'jot down'. Collect phrasal verbs.

words.sentence_ru: Let me write down your number.
words.notes: 'jot down' = quickly write.
words.image_prompt: a hand writing a note on paper

words.meaning: To study a topic by reading
words.sentence_ru: I read up on the topic before the trip.
words.notes: Implies thorough preparation.
words.image_prompt: a person researching by reading several books

tip: Communication verbs at B1: 'mention', 'point out', 'explain'. They make you sound articulate.

words.notes: 'Don't mention it' = you're welcome.
words.image_prompt: two people in casual conversation

words.notes: 'It makes sense' = I understand/it's logical.
words.image_prompt: a person nodding with a clear understanding

tip: Modals of ability/desire: 'be able to', 'manage to', 'feel like'. B1 expands beyond can/want.

words.meaning: To succeed in doing something difficult
words.sentence_ru: I managed to finish on time.
words.notes: Implies effort/challenge overcome.
words.image_prompt: a person succeeding at a challenging task

words.notes: 'feel like + -ing' = want to do.
words.image_prompt: a person craving a warm drink on a cozy afternoon

tip: 'Know' vs 'realize' vs 'figure out' show nuance. B1: 'I reckon', 'I suppose' add natural opinion-hedging.

words.sentence_ru: I just realized I left my keys.
words.notes: 'realise' in UK spelling.
words.image_prompt: a person having a sudden moment of realization

words.notes: 'I reckon' = I think/guess.
words.image_prompt: a person thoughtfully looking at cloudy skies

tip: Beyond good/bad: B1 uses 'decent', 'awful', 'fantastic', 'terrible'. Strong adjectives don't take 'very' ('very awful' is wrong).

words.notes: Understated praise, very common.
words.image_prompt: a pleasant satisfactory meal at a casual restaurant

words.notes: Don't say 'very awful' — it's already extreme.
words.image_prompt: heavy storm clouds over a gloomy landscape

tip: Size with nuance: 'tiny', 'huge', 'enormous'. These 'extreme adjectives' replace 'very big/small'.

words.sentence_ru: They live in a huge house.
words.notes: 'absolutely huge' (not 'very huge').
words.image_prompt: a small person beside an enormous mansion

words.notes: 'tiny' already means very small.
words.image_prompt: a tiny kitten curled up in a teacup

tip: 'New' vs 'brand-new' vs 'second-hand'. B1: compound adjectives ('brand-new') add precision.

words.notes: Hyphenated compound adjective.
words.image_prompt: a shiny brand-new car with a ribbon

words.notes: 'used' is a synonym. UK: 'second-hand shop'.
words.image_prompt: a charming vintage shop with pre-owned goods

tip: Describing things positively: 'gorgeous', 'stunning', 'fascinating'. B1 moves beyond 'nice' and 'interesting'.

words.notes: Stronger than 'pretty'. Very common.
words.image_prompt: a breathtakingly beautiful sunset over a lake

words.notes: 'fascinated' (feeling) vs 'fascinating' (cause).
words.image_prompt: a person utterly absorbed in a fascinating discovery

tip: Time expressions are B1 gold: 'at dawn', 'overnight', 'around midday'. Prepositions of time (at/in/on) need care.

words.notes: 'at dawn', 'at dusk' (evening).
words.image_prompt: soft first light of dawn over rolling hills

words.sentence_ru: We stayed overnight in a hotel.
words.sentence_en: For the duration of a night.
words.notes: Also adjective: 'an overnight trip'.
words.image_prompt: a peaceful starry night over a quiet inn

tip: Parts of day take specific prepositions: 'in the morning', 'at night'. B1 should master these collocations.

words.sentence_ru: I'll call you first thing tomorrow.
words.notes: 'first thing in the morning' = very early.
words.image_prompt: early morning sunlight on a fresh new day

words.meaning: To relax, especially in the evening
words.sentence_ru: I wind down with a book at night.
words.notes: Opposite of 'gear up'.
words.image_prompt: a person relaxing peacefully in the evening

tip: Relative time: 'the day before yesterday', 'the other day', 'sooner or later'. These natural phrases mark B1+.

words.sentence_ru: I saw her the other day.
words.notes: Doesn't specify exactly when.
words.image_prompt: a warm recent memory captured in soft focus

words.sentence_ru: You'll find out sooner or later.
words.notes: Common fixed idiom.
words.image_prompt: a winding path leading toward an inevitable horizon

words.sentence_ru: From now on, I'll exercise daily.
words.notes: Signals a resolution/change.
words.image_prompt: a sunrise marking a fresh determined beginning

tip: Weekday collocations: 'on Monday', 'every Tuesday', 'by Friday'. Prepositions with days are a B1 staple.

words.notes: 'on weekdays' = Mon–Fri.
words.image_prompt: a busy weekday morning commute scene

words.notes: Wednesday is 'hump day' (US slang).
words.image_prompt: a calm Wednesday afternoon coffee break

tip: 'Deadline', 'due', 'put off' relate to scheduling — practical B1 vocabulary for work and study.

words.meaning: Expected or required by a time
words.sentence_ru: The report is due on Thursday.
words.notes: 'due to' also means 'because of'.
words.image_prompt: a calendar with an important deadline marked

words.sentence_ru: Let's put off the meeting till Friday.
words.notes: Also: 'put off' = discourage someone.
words.image_prompt: a meeting being rescheduled on a planner

words.sentence_ru: I look forward to the weekend.
words.notes: Followed by noun or -ing, NOT base verb.
words.image_prompt: a person eagerly anticipating an exciting event

tip: Weekend language is casual and warm: 'lie in', 'chill out', 'get away'. Phrasal verbs dominate leisure talk.

words.sentence_ru: I love a lie-in on Sundays.
words.notes: Noun: 'a lie-in'. US: 'sleep in'.
words.image_prompt: a person enjoying a lazy morning in bed

words.meaning: To go on a short trip / escape
words.sentence_ru: We're getting away for the weekend.
words.notes: Noun: 'a getaway' = a short trip.
words.image_prompt: a relaxing weekend escape to a scenic spot

tip: Indirect questions are more polite: 'Could you tell me who...?' vs 'Who is...?'. A key B1 politeness upgrade.

words.meaning: To want to know; to ask oneself
words.notes: 'I was wondering if...' = very polite request.
words.image_prompt: a person gazing thoughtfully, lost in curiosity

words.sentence_ru: I can't figure out what he means.
words.notes: 'work out' is a synonym.
words.image_prompt: a person solving a puzzle with focused concentration

tip: Place and time questions get richer at B1: 'whereabouts', 'how long', 'how come'. Natural variants of basic Wh-words.

words.notes: Softer and chattier than 'where'.
words.image_prompt: a friendly conversation about someone's location

words.notes: Followed by normal word order, not inversion!
words.image_prompt: a person asking a casual curious question

tip: 'Why' and 'how' lead to reasoning words: 'therefore', 'that's why', 'the reason is'. B1 connects ideas smoothly.

words.sentence_ru: The reason I'm late is traffic.
words.notes: 'the reason why' is common but 'the reason' suffices.
words.image_prompt: a person explaining a cause clearly

words.sentence_ru: It rained — that's why we stayed in.
words.notes: Connects two ideas naturally.
words.image_prompt: a clear cause-and-effect illustration

tip: At B1, numbers get practical: ordinals ('first', 'second'), 'a couple', 'dozens'. Approximations sound natural.

words.sentence_ru: I'll be there in a couple of minutes.
words.notes: Vaguer and more natural than 'two'.
words.image_prompt: two items shown together casually

words.sentence_ru: This is my first time here.
words.notes: 1st. Ordinals: first, second, third...
words.image_prompt: a winner standing first on a podium

words.notes: 'twice' = two times; then 'three times'.
words.image_prompt: a single special moment marked on a calendar

tip: Quantifiers are B1 essentials: 'several', 'a few', 'plenty of', 'a bit of'. Countable vs uncountable rules apply.

words.meaning: More than two but not many
words.sentence_ru: I've read several of his books.
words.notes: More than 'a couple', fewer than 'many'.
words.image_prompt: several books stacked on a desk

words.notes: Works with countable & uncountable nouns.
words.image_prompt: an abundant overflowing basket of supplies

words.sentence_ru: I need a bit of help.
words.notes: 'a bit of' + uncountable; 'a few' + countable.
words.image_prompt: a small helping of something offered

tip: Estimating is a real-world B1 skill: 'roughly', 'around', 'or so', 'dozens of'. Vague numbers sound natural.

words.notes: 'about', 'around', 'approximately' are synonyms.
words.image_prompt: an estimate shown with an approximate gauge

words.notes: A dozen = 12; 'dozens' = loosely many.
words.image_prompt: dozens of items spread across a surface

words.sentence_ru: I'll need an hour or so.
words.notes: 'ten or so' = about ten.
words.image_prompt: a clock showing an approximate amount of time

words.sentence_ru: It'll take ten minutes at most.
words.notes: Opposite: 'at least'.
words.image_prompt: a measuring scale showing a maximum limit

tip: English is global. Knowing how to talk about countries, accents, and varieties (British, American, Australian) is a B1+ asset.

words.meaning: Someone who speaks a language from birth
words.sentence_ru: He's a native speaker of English.
words.notes: 'non-native' = learned later.
words.image_prompt: a friendly person speaking naturally and confidently

words.meaning: Able to speak smoothly and easily
words.notes: 'fluent in X'. Your B1 goal: fluency!
words.image_prompt: a confident person communicating smoothly with others
